For an ex-Governor General returning to Europe, on resignation, a steamer belonging to the Indian Marine Service will (if consistent with the requirements of the public service) be gratuitously provided, to convey him and his family and suite to Suez; but the homeward voyage of the ship thus provided shall not be continued beyond Suez. The ex-Governor General will bear the cost of entertainment and messing incurred while on board, in respect of himself and his party, according to the rules in force relating to the conveyance of passengers in Indian Marine troopships.
  5. 5. No grant from Indian revenues will be made to any ex-Governor General for his homeward journey from Suez.
  6. 6. An ex-Governor General returning to Europe may be accompanied by two officers of his personal staff, for whom free passages to England will be allowed according to the rules in the Military Department, and also free return passages to India, if they start on their return voyage within two months from the date of quitting India.
  7. 7. In the event of a Governor General dying and leaving a widow in India, a steamer belonging to the Indian Marine Service will (if consistent with the requirements of the public service) be provided to convey her to Suez in like manner as in the case of an ex-Governor General. The widow of a deceased Governor General so returning to Europe may be accompanied by one officer of the personal staff on the conditions mentioned in the preceding rule, and shall bear all such expenses as would, in ordinary course, be borne by an ex-Governor General returning to Europe.
II.

8. The allowances for equipment and voyage of the Governors of Madras and Bombay, the Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India, of Madras, and of Bombay, if resident in Europe at the time of appointment respectively, are fixed as follows:—

£
The Governor of Madras ..... 1,000
The Governor of Bombay ..... 1,000
The Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India ..... 500
The Commander-in-Chief, Madras..... 500
The Commander-in-Chief, Bombay ... 500

9. A Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India proceeding from Europe to assume his command may be accompanied by three combatant military officers for his personal staff, and newly-appointed Governors of Madras and of Bombay, and Commanders-in-Chief of Madras and of Bombay proceeding from Europe to take up their duties, may respectively be accompanied by two combatant military officers for their personal staff, the passages of these officers being provided at the expense of Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department as to military officers proceeding to India on duty; but with these exceptions, Government will make no arrangements for, and will defray none of the expenses connected with, the passage or conveyance of any of the officers mentioned in Rule 8, or of their families to or from India.

10. An ex-Governor of Madras or Bombay, or an ex-Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India or of Madras or Bombay, or the widow of a Governor or of a Commander-in-Chief who may die in India leaving a widow there, returning to Europe, may be accompanied by one officer of his personal staff, for whom a free passage will be allowed by Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department, and also a free return passage if he starts on the return voyage within two months from the date of quitting India.

III.

11. The allowances for equipment and voyage of Members of the Council of the Governor General of India, and of Bishops of Calcutta, of Madras, and of Bombay, if resident in Europe at the time of appointment respectively, are fixed as follows:—

£
Members of the Council of the Governor General of India..... 300
The Bishop of Calcutta..... 300
The Bishop of Madras ..... 300
The Bishop of Bombay ..... 300

12. An officer of any of the Indian Services, temporarily residing in Europe, or a military officer transferred from one superior command in India to another during temporary residence out of India, shall not be deemed to be resident in Europe at the time of appointment, within the meaning of Rule 8 or Rule 11.

IV.

RULES made under Statute 24 and 25 Vict, Ch 104, Sect. 6, by the Secretary of State in Council of India, fixing the expenses for equipment and voyage of the Chief Justice and Judges of the several High Courts established under the said Statute, dated the 31st January, 1888.

39. For the purposes of defraying the expenses of appointment and voyage from Europe on first appointment there shall be allowed—

To a Chief Justice or Judge of any High Court £300
But no such allowance shall be made to any person who, being in India, is appointed to the office of Chief Justice or Judge, or who, having been in India, is in Europe at the time of his appointment with the intention of returning to India.

REGULATIONS FOR ADMISSION TO THE INDIAN STAFF CORPS.*

1. A certain number of appointments to the Indian Staff Corps will be offered to candidates for admission to the Royal Military College at each Further Examination, and to officers of the Militia (not over 23 years of age) at each Competitive Examination.

2. All Queen's cadets (British and Indian) and Honorary Queen's cadets nominated by the Secretary of State for India in Council will have the option of electing whether they will join the Royal Military College for appointment to the Staff Corps, or for Commissions in British Cavalry or Infantry. The appointments in the Staff Corps remaining after the claims of the Queen's Cadets and Honorary Queen's Cadets (Indian) have been satisfied will be allotted in order of merit to successful candidates who have elected for the Indian Staff Corps at the Sandhurst Further and Militia Competitive Examinations.

3. Election shall be final, and no candidate for the Staff Corps will thereafter be eligible for transfer to the Cavalry or Infantry of the Line except by exchange.

4. After the final examination at the Royal Military College, and in the case of Militia candidates after the Competitive Examination, a successful candidate will be gazetted as Second Lieutenant on the unattached List of the British Army, and will be provided with passage to India. He will be required to embark within two months of being gazetted. From the date of first commission to the date of arrival in India he will receive the British pay of his rank.

5. On arrival in India he will be attached to a British regiment, and will receive Indian pay and allowances as an officer of the British Army. While so attached, leave (excepting privilege leave) may only be granted to him on medical certificate, and during any leave out of India he will be restricted to the British pay of his rank.

6. At the expiration of one year's actual duty with a British regiment (exclusive of all leave excepting privilege leave) he will be admitted to the Staff Corps in the rank of Second Lieutenant and appointed to a Native regiment.

7. At the expiration of one year's actual duty with a Native regiment (exclusive of all leave excepting privilege leave) he will be promoted to the rank of Lieutenant, provided he has passed the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard. Should he not then have passed the examination he will only be promoted from date of passing. An exception to this rule may be made, with the sanction of the Secretary of State for India, in the case of delay or illness resulting from field service.

8. He will not be permitted to remain in the Service unless during the first three years from date of first commission his retention therein is shown to be in very respect desirable.

9. Within three years of the date of his admission to the Staff Corps he will be required to pass the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under Indian regulations.

10. Until he shall have passed the above examinations he will not be eligible to hold any permanent or officiating appointment (regimental or other) except that of Squadron or Wing Officer.

11. Should he not pass the examinations within the period of three years, he will be removed from the Service and be provided with passage to Europe; he may also be allowed, at the discretion of the Government of India, a gratuity not exceeding one year's British pay of his rank.

12. No extension of the period within which the examinations must be passed will be allowed except on account of delay or illness resulting from employment on field service, and only then with the special sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

13. Any officer of the British Army who may be specially selected for exceptional qualifications for service in India may, with the concurrence of the Secretary of State for War, be admitted to the Indian Staff Corps.

14. An exchange may be permitted between an officer of the British Service and an officer of the Staff Corps, provided both officers have under seven years' service, that the officer exchanging into the Staff Corps is fully qualified for staff employ under Indian regulations, that immediate and suitable employment is available for him, and that he is physically fit for service in India.

15. Under special circumstances or in special cases the Secretary of State for India in Council may deviate from any of the above regulations.

ADMISSION OF OFFICERS ADMITTED TO THE ARMY BEFORE THE 1ST SEPTEMBER,

1892.

PART I.

Officers serving in India.

  1. 1. An officer serving in India will be eligible to become a Probationer on the following conditions:—
    1. I. He shall, at time of application, have completed one year's actual regimental duty whether in or out of India.
    2. II. He shall not, at date of appointment to a Native regiment, be more than 25 years of age.
  2. 2. His application must be accompanied by certificates in the Forms A 1 or A 2 and B, and by a declaration, in duplicate, in the Form C.

* These regulations will in the first instance apply to candidates for admission to the Royal Military College who pass the Further Examination in June, 1891, and to the Militia officers who pass the Competitive Examination for commissions in the Army in September 1892. Officers admitted to the Army before the 1st September, 1892, will remain eligible for appointment to the Staff Corps, to such extent as may be determined, under the regulations which follow (see page 5).

  1. 3. The period of probation must be passed with Native troops, and the date of joining a Native Corps shall be the date of appointment on probation.
  2. 4. Should the officer not have passed the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard before joining a Native Corps, he will be required to do so within 18 months of so joining, otherwise he will cease to be a Probationer.
  3. 5. After the expiration of one year from the date of appointment on probation, the Probationer, provided he has passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation, may be admitted to the Staff Corps.
  4. 6. On the expiration of 18 months from the date of appointment on probation he will be admitted to the Staff Corps whether he has passed the above examinations or not, on production of certificates in the Forms B, C and D.
  5. 7. Should he not have passed the above examinations before admission to the Staff Corps, he will be allowed a further period of 18 months (or three years in all from date of appointment on probation) within which to pass.
  6. 8. Should he not pass within this period he will be removed from the Service.
PART II.Officers of Corps serving out of India.*
  1. 9. An officer of a corps serving out of India will be eligible to become a Probationer on the following conditions:
    1. I. He shall, at time of application, have completed one year's actual regimental duty; in the case of officers of the West India regiment, two years.
    2. II. He shall not, on the 1st of January of the trooping season during which he embarks for India, be more than 25 years of age.
  2. 10.† His application must be submitted through his commanding officer to the Military Secretary, Horse Guards, and be accompanied by certificates in the Forms B and E, and by a declaration in the Form C.
  3. 11. If accepted, a candidate will be required to embark for India within two months of being so warned, and the date of embarkation for India shall be the date of his coming on the Indian Establishment.
  4. 12. The period of probation must be passed with Native troops; the candidate will be appointed to a Native Corps on first arrival in India, and the date of joining it will be the date of his appointment on probation.
  5. 13. Before the expiration of 18 months from date of joining a Native Corps, he must pass the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard, otherwise he will cease to be a Probationer.
  6. 14. After the expiration of one year from date of joining a Native Corps, the Probationer, provided he has passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation, may be admitted to the Staff Corps.
  7. 15. On the expiration of 18 months from the date of joining a Native Corps, he will be admitted to the Staff Corps, whether he has passed the above examinations or not, on production of certificates in the Forms B, C and D.
  8. 16. Should he not have passed the above examinations before admission to the Staff Corps, he will be allowed a further period of 18 months (or three years in all from date of joining a Native Corps) within which to pass.
  9. 17. Should he not pass within this period, he will be removed from the Service.
PART III.General Regulations.
  1. 18. The names of Probationers will be retained on the lists of their British regiments until they are admitted to the Staff Corps.
  2. 19. During the period of probation it will be open to the Commanding Officer of a Native Corps to report that he thinks the Officer, either from temper or other cause, is unsuited for employment with Native troops; in which case the Commander-in-Chief in India, should he be satisfied that such is the case, will post the Officer to his own regiment or to a battalion of it, if one be serving in India, or, if not, to any regiment or battalion in India pending orders from His Royal Highness Commander-in-Chief.‡
  3. 20. The same course will be followed in the case of an officer who may not, at the expiration of 18 months' duty be able to obtain the necessary certificates for admission to the Staff Corps.
  4. 21. No Officer will be allowed to resign his probationary appointment except on sufficient grounds to be approved by the Commander-in-Chief in India, the Government of India, the Secretary of State for India in Council, and his Royal Highness the Commander-in-Chief. An officer applying for permission to resign will be required to produce a declaration and a certificate in the Forms F and G, and the Commander-in-Chief in India, if satisfied with the reason given, will at once post the officer to his own regiment or to a battalion of it, if one be serving in India, or, if not, to any regiment or battalion of the line in that country, his resignation being sent forward at once for the final order of the Secretary of State for India and His Royal Highness the Commander-in-Chief.
  5. 22. A Probationer obtaining leave out of India either on private affairs, or for more than six months on medical certificate, will be held to have resigned his probationary appointment.

* These Regulations are also applicable generally to officers of the Royal Marines.

† The application of an officer of Marines must be addressed to the Admiralty.

‡ An officer of Royal Marines ceasing to be a Probationer will not be attached to a British Regiment in India, but will at once be provided with passage to England, and will revert to the disposal of the Admiralty on arrival in that country.

23. Until the completion of three years' service a Probationer shall remain subject to the provisions of the Royal Warrant of 5 Dec. 1888.*

24. When an Officer is ultimately admitted to the Staff Corps the date assigned to his admission shall be that of his first appointment on probation.

25. An Officer shall not be eligible to hold any permanent or officiating appointment, regimental or other (except that of Squadron or Wing Officer), until he shall have passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation.

26. No extension of the prescribed period within which these examinations must be passed will be allowed except on account of illness or delay resulting from employment on field service, and only then with the special sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

27. An Officer removed from the Service under para. 8 or para. 17 will receive a free passage to Europe and a gratuity of one year's English pay of his rank.

28. Under special circumstances, or in special cases, the Secretary of State for India in Council may deviate from any of the above regulations.

CERTIFICATES AND DECLARATIONS.

A 1.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving in India.
(Para. 2.)

Certified that Lieutenant _____ of the _____ regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed _____ years' service from the date of his first commission, of which _____ years were (or the whole period of which was, as the case may be) spent in India.

Lieutenant _____ [†has passed an examination in the native languages by the higher (or lower) standard, and] has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or † Artillery) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop, or battery) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant _____ has served with th's regiment, he has been attentive to his duties, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

A 2.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—Alternative which may be used for appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving in India of an Officer of short service. (Para. 2.)

Certified that Lieutenant _____ of the _____ regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed _____ years' service from the date of his first commission, of which _____ years were (or the whole period was, as the case may be), spent in India.

Lieutenant _____ [†has passed an examination in the Native languages by the Higher (or Lower) standard] has passed his drills, and has performed in a satisfactory manner his duties as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or † Artillery). He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant _____ has served with this regiment he has been attentive to his duty, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

B.

Medical Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer and for admission. Paras. 2, 6, 10, and 15.)

I certify that to the best of my belief Lieutenant _____ is in good bodily health, and apparently well capable of undergoing the vicissitudes of service in India, that his vision is equal to the standard required for officers on entering the service, and that there is no defect in his hearing that would be likely to interfere with the proper performance of his duties as an officer of Cavalry (or Infantry).

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the medical officer of the regiment.)

* "An officer shall not be permitted to remain in Our Army unless during the first three years of his service his retention therein is known to be in every respect desirable."

† To be inserted if the Officer has so passed.

‡ Horse, field, or garrison, as the case may be.

C.

Declaration of Candidate.—For appointment as Probationer. (Paras. 2, 6, 10, and 15.)

I hereby declare, upon my honour as an Officer and a gentleman, that I have not, nor to the best of my belief has any person on my behalf or in my interest, directly or indirectly, received or agreed to receive any sum of money, valuable security, or other thing in respect of my appointment to the Indian Staff Corps; and I promise, honourably and unreservedly that I will not receive the same or in any manner recognize or benefit by its receipt hereafter.

(Signature of candidate.)

[The above declaration must be drawn up in the officer's own handwriting, and must be read over in the presence of the officer commanding the regiment, who will attach his certificate as witness to the declaration in the following manner:—]

Read over in my presence, and signed before me this day of

18

(Signature of commanding officer.)

[The declaration must also be subscribed by a second witness, who may be either an officer of standing in the service, or a civilian, being a householder.]

D.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For admission to the Staff Corps. (Paras. 6 and 15.)

Certified that Lieutenant of the regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed years' service from the date of his first commission, of which years were (or the whole period of which was, as the case may be) spent in India.

Lieutenant has passed an examination in the native languages by the Higher (or Lower) standard, and has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, of the Native Articles of War, and of the Queen's Regulations, as well as of the India Army Regulations, besides having gained some general acquaintance with the organization and nature of all branches of the Indian Army.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duties, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

E.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving out of India. (Para. 10.)

Certified that Lieutenant of the Regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed years' service from the date of his first commission.

Lieutenant has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an Officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or Artillery) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop, or battery) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duty, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

F.

Declaration of Candidate.—For resignation of probationary appointment. (Para. 21.)

I hereby solemnly declare, on my honour as an officer and a gentleman, that my resignation of my probationary appointment for the Staff Corps does not originate in any cause affecting my honour, or my character, or professional efficiency

(Station and date.)

(Signature of probationer.)

G.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For resignation of probationary appointment. (Para. 21.)

I hereby certify that the resignation by Lieutenant of his probationary appointment in the regiment under my command does not originate in any cause affecting his honour, or his fitness to continue in Her Majesty's service.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of commanding officer.)

EXAMINATION IN THE NATIVE LANGUAGES BY THE LOWER STANDARD.

The following are the tests laid down for this examination :—

BENGAL AND BOMBAY.
  • (a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy not less than an ordinary octavo page of the Bágh-o-Bahár.
  • (b.) Translating accurately and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary octavo page of plain English into language similar to that of the Bágh-o-Bahár, in the Persian character.
  • (c.) Reading fairly and translating readily and correctly a Hindustani manuscript written in the Persian character.
  • (d.) (i.) Conversing with the examiner with fluency and with such correctness of pronunciation, grammar, and idiom as to be at once intelligible.
  • (ii.) Conversing as above in the presence of the examiner with a native of the country.
MADRAS.
  • (a.) Reading fairly, and construing with accuracy, not less than half an octavo page of the undermentioned work, the passages being selected by the examining officer himself before the examination—
    1. Bágh-o-Bahár, the second darvesh, including the story of King Azád Bakt.
  • (b.) Conversing with an educated Native in the presence of the examiner on subjects likely to occur in the performance of regimental or professional duty, in the transaction of ordinary business, or in the course of every-day life.
EXAMINATION IN THE NATIVE LANGUAGES BY THE HIGHER STANDARD.

The following are the tests laid down for this examination :—

BENGAL AND BOMBAY.
  • (a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy, not less than an ordinary octavo page of the selections from the prose of the Prem Ságar.
  • (b.) Translating accurately, and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary octavo page of plain English into language similar to that of the Prem Ságar in the Hindi character.
  • (c.) Reading fairly and translating readily and correctly Hindustani manuscripts written in the Hindi character.
  • (d.) Vivá voce translation into the Oriental language of a paper of English sentences, and conversing with the examiners, or in their presence with persons selected by them, with fluency and such correctness of pronunciation, grammar, and idiom as to be at once intelligible.
MADRAS.
  • (a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy, not less than an ordinary octavo page of the Bágh-o-Bahár.
  • (b.) Translating accurately, and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary page of plain English into the Hindustani language.
  • (c.) Reading fairly, and translating readily and correctly, Hindustani manuscripts.
  • (d.) Translating from English into Hindustani a paper of short sentences, the greater part on professional subjects, to be rendered accurately and idiomatically.
  • (e.) Translating vivá voce into Hindustani the proceedings of a court-martial—a duty that will frequently be required of the candidates. (In translating the charge, finding, and sentence, the candidate will be allowed the use of the record; the evidence will be read out by the examiner and translated by the candidate to the natives in attendance. The examiners should satisfy themselves that the candidate's interpretation is clearly and correctly understood.)
  • (f.) Conversing with natives with fluency, and with such correctness of pronunciation, grammar and idiom as to be at once intelligible, and in like manner to understand readily all that the natives say.
PROFESSIONAL EXAMINATION FOR ADMISSION TO THE STAFF CORPS.

The following are the tests for admission to the Staff Corps in virtue of qualifying appointments in Native regiments :—

  • (a.) The evolutions of a regiment of cavalry or a battalion of infantry (as the case may be) as laid down in the regulations of those services respectively, including skirmishing, duties of outposts, patrols, escorts, advanced and rear-guards.
  • (b.) The charge of a troop, company (as the case may be), or detachment in every position in which it may be placed, musketry instruction, requisitions, returns, and correspondence.
  • (c.) The Indian articles of war and the Army Regulations, India, particularly all such as affect the conduct of native troops, their pay, pension, clothing, &c.
  • (d.) The system of regimental interior economy pursued in the Native cavalry and infantry, and especially in

the regiment to which the officer has been attached, including the mode of investigating and dealing with offences, complaints, and petitions from the men, method of keeping rosters for furlough and guard, deductions made from the men's pay and system of accounts, together with a knowledge of every article of equipment used by the men, their cost, and the mode of carrying them.

In the case of probationers for the Native cavalry the examination will include the additional subjects under head (c).

(c.) System of purchase of horses, and the districts whence they are usually obtained, their prevailing defects and good qualities, stable management, general knowledge of farriery, and the diseases and injuries to which horses are liable, with their treatment.

The committee will satisfy themselves by examination in the field and viva voce questions that the officer is instructed in the required subjects.

EXAMINATION FOR REGIMENTAL AND ARMY STAFF APPOINTMENTS.*

Officers of the Staff Corps are not qualified to hold the appointments of Commandant, Second in Command, or Wing Commander, nor to leave their regiments for the purpose of holding any appointment whatever, except that of aide-de-camp (unless they come within the provisions of the following paragraphs), until they shall have passed an examination in the following subjects:—

PART I.

(a.)—Cavalry—

In the field:—

Command of a regiment in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Maneuvring on varied ground (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object)

b.)—Infantry—

Equitation.

In the field:—

Command of a battalion in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division

Skirmishing (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object).

PART II.

(a.) Military law.

(b.) Tactics.

(c.) Field fortification.

(d.) Military topography.

Officers may be nominated at any time to staff appointments for distinguished service in the field, although they may not have passed the qualifying examination; but officers so appointed, who are liable to examination under this order, will be required to qualify within a reasonable time.

All officers in the Staff Corps who may have passed the final examination at the Staff College, will be exempted from the whole of the tests laid down.

PROMOTION.

Under Royal Warrant promotion in the Staff Corps is governed by such regulations as the Secretary of State for India may make from time to time.

The existing regulations are—

The promotion of Second Lieutenants from the Unattached List is governed by Rule 7 of the Regulations for Admission (page 4);

Second Lieutenants appointed to the Army before 1st September, 1892, are promoted to the rank of Lieutenant on permanent transfer to the Staff Corps;

Officers after 11 years' service, of which four must have been in the Staff Corps, become Captains;

After 20 years' service, of which six must have been in the Staff Corps, they become Majors; and

After 26 years service, of which eight must have been in the Staff Corps, they become Lieutenant-Colonels.

The periods of four, six, and eight years' service in the Staff Corps are exclusive of all leave of absence except privilege leave and leave rendered necessary by wounds or illness contracted on service in the field.

* The qualifications required and examinations to be passed for the judge advocate general's department, the administrative departments of the army, the survey department, and for civil and political employ, will be found in the Indian Army Regulations.

A Lieutenant-Colonel becomes eligible for promotion to the rank of Colonel in the Army after three years' satisfactory service with that rank. Selection for promotion will be made under such regulations as the Secretary of State in Council may lay down.

Officers above the rank of Lieutenant are also eligible for promotion by brevet as a reward for distinguished service in the field, or for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

Promotion to the ranks of Major-General and Lieutenant-General will only be made by selection to fill appointments, or as a reward for distinguished service. There will be an establishment of 3 Generals.

Officers in civil employ, after ten years' absence from military duty, are removed from the effective list of the army, and placed on a supernumerary list, rising thereon, under the regulations in force, to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, but to no higher rank.

This condition is, however, not applicable to officers of rank taken for high political or civil employment, or to officers taken for temporary civil or political duties in the field or in newly-acquired territories.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

The claims to pension of the widows and families of officers of the Staff Corps (excluding those who entered the Staff Corps from the late Indian Army) are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pension to the widows and families of officers of H. M.'s British Forces, as may be in force for the time being.

The following are the rates of ordinary pension :—

Pension to Widow. Compassionate Allowance to each Child.
Yearly. Yearly.
General Officer, including Brigadier-General £120 £20
Lieutenant-Colonel or Brevet-Colonel 90 16
Major 70 14
Captain 50 12
Lieutenant 40 10

with increased pensions in the case of the widows and children of officers who have been killed in action or have died of wounds or illness contracted on field service.

Compassionate allowances to sons cease at 18 years of age, and to daughters at 21 years of age or marriage. Widows and families are also entitled to pensions from the Indian Military Service Family Pension Fund, to which all officers must subscribe as a condition of their appointment to the Staff Corps.

The following are the rates of widows' pension :—

In Europe. In India.
Yearly.
£		 Yearly.
Rs.
Lieutenant-Colonel of five years' service in the rank (if subscribed for)† 160 1,669
Lieutenant-Colonel 130 1,356
Major 100 1,043
Captain 70 730
Lieutenant 40 417

Children's pensions (all ranks alike) dependent on age :—
In Europe from £10 to £45 per annum.
In India from Rs. 104 to Rs. 469 „

Pensions to sons cease at 21 years of age, to daughters on marriage.

ROYAL WARRANT FOR THE PROMOTION AND TRANSFER TO THE UNEMPLOYED SUPERNUMERARY LIST OF OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN ARMY.

VICTORIA, R. I.,

Whereas it has been represented to Us that it is expedient to amend the Regulations for governing the Promotion in the Army of the Officers of Our Indian Forces, and their transfer to the Unemployed Supernumary List, Our will and pleasure is, that, with effect from the 1st January, 1891, the following Regulations be established, and be the sole Regulations for these purposes viz. :—

1. A Lieutenant-Colonel may attain the rank of Colonel—
(a) By twelve years' service in the substantive grade of Lieutenant-Colonel in the "local service."
(b) On being selected for the rank of Colonel under such regulations as our Secretary of State for India in Council may lay down; provided he shall have completed three years' satisfactory service as a Lieutenant-Colonel.

(c) By brevet, on appointment to be one of Our Aides-de-Camp.

(d) By brevet, as a reward for distinguished service in the field.

(e) By brevet, for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

2. If it shall be necessary to appoint an Officer to a situation which may be declared by Our Secretary of State for India in Council to be one which shall carry the rank of Colonel, although the Officer may not have the rank or service to qualify for promotion, the local and temporary rank of Colonel only shall, until he shall have completed the necessary qualifying service, be conferred on him while holding such appointment.

3. The rank of Brigadier-General shall be local or temporary only.

4. The names of General Officers shall be borne upon four separate lists. The first shall contain the names of

* This does not refer to the special rates of half-pay of unemployed General Officers who are effective officers.

† Subscription for this pension is voluntary.

those on the active list, and of those who, having been promoted out of their regular turn shall be supernumerary; the second, the names of Officers retired under Our Royal Warrant of the 31st December, 1877; the third, the names of Officers placed on the unemployed supernumerary list under Our Royal Warrant of the 24th October, 1881; and the fourth the names of those who shall have retired from the service.

5. There shall be no establishment of General Officers, and after the number of General Officers has been reduced, and the names of the Staff Corps Officers removed from the list of the Indian Army, as provided in Articles 6 and 7, there shall be no more than one General. Subject to this limitation, a Lieutenant-General of the Indian Army shall be promoted to the rank of General when the Lieutenant-General of the Staff Corps next below him in seniority as a Lieutenant-General is promoted to the rank of General in ordinary course, or, if there be already a General of the Indian Army, so soon thereafter as a casualty may occur. The maximum number (not to be exceeded except during a period of war) of Lieutenant-Generals shall be three, and that of Major-Generals shall be thirteen, and promotion to those grades shall be made under such regulations as Our Secretary of State for India in Council may lay down.

6. The names of the Colonels and General Officers of the Staff Corps now borne on the Indian List to regulate promotion shall remain on that list (to be thereon retained in italics, until their death, retirement, or transfer to the unemployed supernumerary list, for the purpose of regulating the promotion of the Officers below them), until the number of General Officers has been reduced as provided in Article 7. The names of the Generals and Lieutenant-Generals shall be removed when the number of Lieutenant-Generals has been reduced, and the names of the Major-Generals and Colonels when the number of Major-Generals has been reduced.

7. After the 31st December, 1890, the number of General Officers on the active list shall be gradually reduced as follows:—

  • (a) Officers who were on the establishment of General Officers on the 31st December, 1886, shall be promoted to vacancies in the rank of Lieutenant-General as casualties occur.
  • (b) As vacancies occur in the rank of Major-General, the second vacancy in every two shall be absorbed; and when there are no more Officers qualified under (a) for promotion to the rank of Lieutenant-General, the third vacancy of every three in that rank shall also be absorbed. The remaining vacancies shall be filled until the number of Lieutenant-Generals of the Indian Army is reduced to three, and that of Major-Generals to thirteen.

8. Subject to the above reduction, every vacancy in the rank of Major-General shall be filled by the promotion of the senior Colonel of the Indian Army; or by the moving up of the name of the senior Officer of the Staff Corps borne on the list to regulate promotion; or by the absorption of a General Officer promoted for distinguished service. Every vacancy in the rank of Lieutenant-General or in that of General shall be filled up in like manner by the promotion of the Senior Officer of the Indian Army in the grade next below, or by the moving up of the name of the Senior Officer of the Staff Corps borne on the list of that grade.

9. Temporary or local rank and command as Major-General, Lieutenant-General, or General may, for the convenience of Our Service, be conferred on an Officer of the next lower rank (whether he hold such rank permanently or temporarily), without regard to seniority.

10. A Colonel who shall vacate an appointment as temporary Major-General, may upon the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, be made an honorary Major-General.

11. Promotion may be conferred upon a Colonel, Major-General, or Lieutenant-General, without regard to vacancies, for distinguished service in the field, or of an exceptional nature other than in the field, and in such case the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, detailing the services for which the Officer is promoted, shall be published in the General Orders of Our Indian Military Forces, and in the London Gazette.

12. Field Marshals shall be appointed at the will of the Sovereign, and the rank shall be conferred without reference to seniority. A General Officer, retired under Our Warrant of the 31st December, 1877, or placed on the unemployed supernumerary list under Our Warrant of the 24th October, 1881, shall remain eligible for promotion to the rank of Field Marshal.

13. A Major-General or Lieutenant-General, on the unemployed supernumerary list, who was promoted to the former rank not later than the 31st December, 1886, shall be promoted to higher rank when the Officer next junior to him on the effective list is so promoted.

14. Until the number of General Officers has been reduced, as provided in Article 7, a Colonel, a Major-General, or a Lieutenant-General placed on the unemployed supernumerary list shall be promoted to higher rank when the Officer on the establishment who was next junior to him as a Colonel shall obtain such promotion.

15. A Major-General shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list on attaining the age of 62 years, and a Lieutenant-General or General on attaining the age of 67 years.

16. Until the number of General Officers in each grade has been reduced, as provided in Article 7, a General Officer shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list after being unemployed for five years continuously since his promotion to Major-General, or after being unemployed for seven years continuously from the time of his last employment as Colonel.

17. A Colonel of the "local service," that is, an Officer whose appointment was not made subject to the conditions prescribed in the General Order of Our Governor General of India in Council, dated the 9th December, 1859, No. 1637, shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list, provided he is in receipt of Colonel's allowance and not in military employ,—

If he attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel before the 1st October, 1877,—at the age of 58 years.

If he attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel on or after the 1st October, 1877,—at the age of 57 years.

18. A Colonel of the "general list," that is, an Officer whose appointment was made subject to the above-mentioned General Order, shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list on attaining the age of 57 years; a Lieutenant-Colonel at the age of 55 years.

19. In the event of an Officer being called to, or engaged on, active service in the field, at or about the time when, under any article of this Our Warrant, he would be liable to be transferred to the unemployed supernumerary list, Our Secretary of State for India in Council may, on the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, extend his term of service during the continuance of the emergency as a supernumerary to the establishment.

20. If in any special case, not under the terms of the preceding article, it appears desirable to postpone the transfer of an Officer to the unemployed supernumerary list, Our Secretary of State for India in Council may, upon the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, so postpone it.

21. Our Secretary of State for India in Council, upon the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council

UNEMPLOYED SUPERNUMERARY LIST REGULATIONS. lxvii

may also bring back an Officer from the unemployed supernumerary list to the effective list, should it appear essential to the interests of Our Service to do so.

22. An Officer whose case has been specially treated under Articles 19, 20, or 21 shall, so long as he remains on the effective list, be supernumerary thereon.

Given at Our Court at Balmoral, this twenty-seventh day of October, in the fifty-fourth year of Our reign.

By Her Majesty's command.

(Signed) Cross.

REGULATIONS FOR THE TRANSFER OF OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN STAFF CORPS TO THE UNEMPLOYED SUPERNUMERARY LIST.

1. General Officers shall be placed on the Unemployed Supernumerary List on completing the ages and periods of non-employment fixed under Article 89 of the warrant of the 22nd February, 1890, for the compulsory retirement of the General Officers of the British Service.

2. Colonels who entered the Staff Corps on or before the 12th September, 1866, shall be placed on the Unemployed supernumerary list, provided they are in receipt of Colonel's allowance and not in military employ, and Colonels who entered the Staff Corps after that date, whether in receipt of the allowance and employed or not:—

If they attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel before the 1st October, 1877—at the age of 58 years.

If they attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel on or after the 1st October, 1877—at the age of 57 years.

3. Lieutenant-Colonels not selected for promotion to the rank of Colonel will be transferred to the unemployed supernumerary list after attaining the age of 55 years, under the same conditions as apply to Colonels.

4. In special cases, where it may appear desirable, the Secretary of State in Council may delay the transfer of an Officer to, or in case of those under the Parliamentary guarantee, bring an Officer back from, the unemployed supernumerary list.

5. The above regulations shall not affect Officers whose admission to the Staff Corps bears date on or after the 1st July, 1891. These Officers come, as regards the limits of age and periods of non-employment, under the retirement articles of the Royal Warrant; the amounts of their pensions, non-effective pay, &c., being governed by Indian Regulations.

ROYAL WARRANT FOR CONTINUOUS SERVICE OF ROYAL ENGINEER OFFICERS IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, R. & I.

Whereas it has been represented to Us that it is expedient to secure, for the purposes of the Government of India, the continuous service on the Indian Establishment of Officers of Our Corps of Royal Engineers.

Our Will and Pleasure is that the following Regulations be established for that purpose, subject, nevertheless, to any modifications which may hereafter appear to us to be expedient.

Regulations.

1. Officers of Our Corps of Royal Engineers may, with the sanction of Our Secretary of State for India in Council, elect to render continuous service to the Government of India, and the election shall be open to (1) Officers who at the date of this Our Warrant are performing their first tour of service in India, or who may hereafter proceed to India for that purpose, and who continue to serve for a second or third tour, without interruption except by leave, the election being made at any time during such service, not later than six months prior to the expiry of their third tour;* (2) Officers who are now serving on the Indian Establishment, but who have served part of their time elsewhere than in India; (3) Officers who having previously served in India are now serving on the British Establishment; (the election in each of the latter classes to be made on or before the 1st October 1886).

2. Officers electing for such continuous service shall enjoy the conditions of Indian pay and allowances at present existing, subject to such modifications of the same as may at any time appear expedient.

3. They shall be eligible for leave under the conditions applicable to Officers admitted to Our Indian Staff Corps on or after the date of this Our Warrant.

4. They shall be subject to the same regulations as to military promotion, and liability to removal from the effective list, as apply to the other Officers of Our Royal Engineers.

5. They shall be required, should occasion arise, to serve elsewhere than on the Indian Establishment.

6. They shall be allowed, under conditions to be laid down by Our Secretary of State for India in Council, and with his sanction and that of Our Secretary of State for War, to exchange with Officers of the Corps serving on the British Establishment.

7. They shall be liable, in view to their becoming acquainted with the progress made in military science and engineering, and to their qualifying themselves for promotion in their Corps, to return to England to do duty at the School of Military Engineering, for such periods and under such conditions as may, from time to time, be prescribed by Our Secretary of State for India in Council in concurrence with Our Secretary of State for War, and such duty shall count as service for Indian pension.

8. The Regulations under which, with the view of securing the flow of promotion in Our Corps of Royal Engineers, Officers of the Corps may be placed on half pay or retired, shall not be held to disqualify the Officers on the Indian Establishment so affected from continuing to hold any appointments in the Civil Departments of the Government of India which they may have been holding previously, and, subject to the discretion of the Government of India, their tenure of such appointments shall be regulated by the rules which apply to other persons holding similar appointments in the same Departments.

9. Officers permitted to elect for continuous Indian service shall be eligible, except as is hereinafter laid down, for pensions under the conditions and at the rates applicable to Officers admitted to Our Staff Corps after the 1st July, 1881.

10. Service for pension shall be reckoned from date of first commission, and it shall include all service on the effective list and on the half pay or retired list provided for in Article 8 of this Our Warrant.

11. An Officer serving on the Indian Establishment at the date of this Our Warrant, who has served part of his time elsewhere than in India, or an Officer serving on the British Establishment at this same date, who may hereafter proceed to India, shall on retirement receive a pension consisting of the retired pay to

* As modified by Royal Warrant of 19th April, 1890.

† Altered to 1st November 1886 by a subsequent Warrant.

which he would be entitled under Our Warrant regulating the retired pay of Our Royal Engineers generally, and, in addition, such a portion of the difference between the said retired pay and the Staff Corps pension belonging to his total length of service as is represented by the proportion his Indian service bears to the minimum total service that would have qualified him for the before-mentioned Staff Corps pension. In calculating the period of Indian service for pension, three years of British service shall be reckoned as Indian service.

12. Should an Officer retire before he has served a sufficient time to entitle him to a pension under the rules applicable to the Staff Corps as aforesaid, he shall be granted only such retired pay or gratuity as he would have been entitled to receive under Our Warrant regulating the retired pay of Officers of Our Royal Engineers generally had he not elected for continuous Indian service.

13. Officers who have already had periods of leave under conditions which allowed them to count such periods as service for pension shall, in the event of their volunteering for permanent service in India, be allowed the benefit of counting such periods towards pension under the conditions of this Our Warrant.

14. Officers of the permanent Indian Establishment transferred to the half pay list on account of ill-health shall come under the same conditions as Officers of the Indian Staff Corps.

15. Our Secretary of State for India in Council shall be the sole administrator and interpreter of this Our Warrant, and shall be empowered to issue such instructions in reference thereto as he may from time to time deem necessary.

Given at Our Court at Windsor, this twentieth day of February 1886, and in the forty-ninth year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command,

KIMBERLEY.

REGULATIONS FOR THE EXAMINATION OF CANDIDATES FOR THE APPOINTMENT OF SURGEON-LIEUTENANT IN HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

1. The regulations are those in force at the present time. They are subject to any alteration that may be determined on.

Limits of age.

2. All natural-born subjects of Her Majesty, between 21 and 28 years of age* at the date of the examination, and of sound bodily health may be candidates. They may be married or unmarried. They must possess a diploma or diplomas entitling them under the Medical Acts to practise both medicine and surgery.

Declaration to be submitted.

3. They must subscribe and send in to the Military Secretary, India Office, Westminster, so as to reach that address at least a fortnight before the date fixed for the Examination, a declaration according to the annexed form.

4. This declaration must be accompanied by the following documents:—

(a) Proof of age either by extract from the register of the parish in which the candidate was born, or where such extract is unattainable, by his own declaration (pursuant to the Act 5 and 6 Will. 4, c. 62), form of which can be obtained at the India Office. A certificate of baptism which does not afford proof of age will be useless.

(b) A recommendation from some person of standing in society—not a member of his own family—to the effect that he is of regular and steady habits, and likely in every respect to prove creditable to the service if admitted; and a certificate of moral character from a magistrate, or a minister of the religious denomination to which the candidate belongs.

(c) A certificate of registration, under the Medical Acts, of the degrees, diplomas, and licenses possessed by the candidate.

Physical Examination.

5. The physical fitness of each candidate will be determined previous to examination by a Board of Medical Officers, who are required to certify that his vision is sufficiently good to enable him to perform any surgical operation without the aid of glasses. A moderate degree of myopia will not be considered a disqualification, provided it does not necessitate the use of glasses during the performance of operations, and that no organic disease of the eyes exists. Every candidate must also be free from all other organic disease, and from constitutional weakness, or other disability likely to unfit him for military service in India.

Subjects for Examination.

6. On producing the foregoing qualifications, and on payment of a fee of £1, the candidate will be examined by the Examining Board in the following compulsory subjects, and the highest number of marks attainable will be distributed as follows:—

Marks.
a. Anatomy and Physiology .. .. . 1,000
b. Surgery .. .. . 1,000
c. Medicine, including Therapeutics, the diseases of women and children .. .. . 1,000
d. Chemistry and Pharmacy, and a practical knowledge of Drugs .. .. . 1,000

N.B.—The examination in medicine and surgery will be in part practical, and will include operations on the dead body, the application of surgical apparatus, and the examination of medical and surgical patients at the bedside. The examination in chemistry will be limited to the elements of the science, and to its application to medicine, pharmacy, and practical hygiene.

No candidate shall be considered eligible who shall not have obtained at least one-third of the marks obtainable in each of the above compulsory subjects.

Voluntary subjects.

7. Candidates may be examined in the following voluntary subjects, for which the maximum number of marks obtainable will be:—

Marks.
French, German, and Hindustani† (150 each) .. .. . 450
Natural Sciences .. .. . 300

The natural sciences will include comparative anatomy, zoology, natural philosophy, physical geography, and botany with special reference to materia medica.

A number less than one third of the marks obtainable in any of these voluntary subjects will not be allowed to count in favour of a candidate who has qualified in the compulsory subjects.

The knowledge of modern languages being considered of great importance, all intending competitors are urged to qualify in French and German.

8. The appointments announced for competition will be filled up from the list of qualified

* But no candidate will be permitted after August, 1891, to compete more than twice.

† Further particulars as to the standard of eyesight will be found in a pamphlet by Sir J. Fayer, M.D., K.C.S.I., etc., entitled "Regulations as to Defects of Vision," etc., published by Messrs. J. & A. Churchill, 11, New Burlington Street, London, W.

‡ Candidates presenting themselves for examination in Hindustani, are recommended to study some recognized text-book, such as the annotated edition of the Taubat-un-Nasuk, which is the text used by the candidates for the Indian Civil Service.

candidates arranged in order of merit, as finally determined by the total number of marks each has obtained in both the compulsory and voluntary subjects.

The Examiners in London will prepare a list in order of merit, with the marks affixed in the different subjects, to be transmitted to the Director-General and communicated to the Professors of the Army Medical School. If any Candidate is found to be deficient in any particular subject, this shall be stated, in order that he may receive special instruction on the point at Netley.

9. After passing this examination, the successful candidates will be required to attend one Course at entire course of practical instruction at the Army Medical School as Surgeons on probation in— Netley.

  1. (1) Hygiene.
  2. (2) Clinical and Military Medicine.
  3. (3) Clinical and Military Surgery.
  4. (4) Pathology of diseases and injuries incident to Military Service.

These courses are to be of not less than four months' duration; but candidates who have already gone through a course at Netley as candidates for the Army or Navy Medical Service may, if thought desirable, be exempted from attending the school a second time.

10. During the period of residence at the Army Medical School, each candidate will Pay while at receive an allowance of 8s. per diem, with quarters, or when quarters are not provided, with Netley. the usual lodging and fuel and light allowances of a subaltern, to cover all costs of maintenance; and he will be required to provide himself with uniform (viz., the regulation undress uniform of a Surgeon of the British service, but without the sword). Uniform while at Netley.

11. All candidates will be required to conform to such rules of discipline as the Senate may from time to time, enact.

12. At the conclusion of the course, candidates will be required to pass an examination on the Examination subjects taught in the School. The examination will be conducted by the Professors of the School. at Netley.

The Director-General, or any Medical Officer deputed by him, may be present and take part in the examination. If the candidate gives satisfactory evidence of being qualified for the practical duties of an Army Medical Officer, he will be eligible for a commission as Surgeon-Lieutenant.

13. The position of the candidates on the list of Surgeon-Lieutenants will be determined by the combined results of the preliminary and of the final examinations, and, so far as the requirements of the service will permit, they will have the choice of Presidency in India, according to their position in that list. Position on List of Surgeon-Lieutenants, how determined.

14. Examinations for admission to the Service usually take place twice in the year, i.e., in February and August. There will be an examination for 17 appointments in February, 1892.

NOTE. Candidates cannot be supplied with past examination papers.

DECLARATION AND SCHEDULE OF QUALIFICATIONS, TO BE FILLED UP BY CANDIDATES,

Christian and Surname at full length.

I, _____ years of age in _____ last, vide accompanying certificate, a Candidate for employment as Surgeon-Lieutenant in Her Majesty's Indian Medical Service, do hereby attest my readiness to engage for that service, and to proceed on duty immediately on being gazetted.

I declare that I labour under no mental nor constitutional disease, nor any imperfection or disability that can interfere with the most efficient discharge of the duties of a Medical Officer.
(The Dates of Graduations and the Universities or Colleges are to be stated.)

  • I have the Degree of A.M. or A.B. from the
  • I have the Degree of M.D. or M.B. from the
  • I have a License to Practise Medicine from the
  • I have a Diploma in Surgery from the
  • I have a License to Practise Surgery from the

The Candidate should here state whether he has served in the Medical Department of the Army or Navy, or in the Subordinate Medical Department in India.

(Signature at full length)

(Date)

(Place of Residence)

Candidates who desire to undergo the voluntary examination are to sign the following declaration:—

It is my intention to undergo the voluntary examination in *

N.B. This paper when filled up is to be returned, under cover, to the Military Secretary, India Office London, S.W.

MEMORANDUM REGARDING THE POSITION OF MEDICAL OFFICERS TO BE APPOINTED TO HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN FORCES.

INDIA OFFICE, June, 1887.

1. This memorandum is based on the regulations in force at the present time. They are subject to any alteration that may be determined on.

PASSAGE TO INDIA.

2. Surgeon-Lieutenants on appointment will, if possible, be provided with passage to India by troopship; but when troopship accommodation is not available, passage at the public expense will be provided by steamer, or a passage allowance will be granted, if preferred. A charge for messing during the voyage will be made at the rate of 2s. a day. This payment will not include the cost of liquors, which will be charged for as extras.

3. All Surgeon-Lieutenants who shall neglect or refuse to proceed to India under the orders of the Secretary of State for India within two months from the date of leaving Netley, will be considered as having forfeited their appointments, unless special circumstances shall justify a departure from this regulation.

* State what subjects will be taken up.

INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

PAY PREVIOUS TO ARRIVAL IN INDIA.

4. The rate of pay drawn by Surgeon-Lieutenants from the date of passing the final examination at the Army Medical School until arrival in India will be 10s. a day. (For rates subsequent to their landing in India, see paras. 16, 17, and 18.)

Pay at the above rate will be issued in this country up to the date of embarkation, and an advance of two months' pay at the same rate will also be made prior to embarkation, which will be adjusted in India in accordance with the rate laid down in para. 14.

GRADES AND PRECEDENCE.

Royal War-
rant, 7th Dec.
1891, Art. 1.

  • 5. The ranks of Medical Officers in the Indian Military Forces are six in number, viz. :—
    • (1.) Surgeon-Major General.
    • (2.) Surgeon-Colonel.
    • (3.) Brigade Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel.
    • (4.) Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel.
    • (5.) Surgeon-Major.
    • (6.) Surgeon-Captain.
    • (7.) Surgeon-Lieutenant.

6. For purposes of precedence and other advantages attaching to the corresponding military rank, officers in the above grades rank according to the rank indicated by the military portion of the title.

PROMOTION.

Royal War-
rant, Art. 3

7. A Surgeon-Lieutenant's commission dates from the day of passing out of the Army Medical School.

8. A Surgeon-Lieutenant is promoted to the rank of Surgeon-Captain on completing three years' full pay Service.

Royal War-
rant, Art. 4.
Royal War-
rant, Arts. 5, 6
and 7.

9. A Surgeon-Captain is promoted to Surgeon-Major on completion of twelve years' full-pay Service.

10. A Surgeon-Major is promoted to the rank of Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel on completing twenty years full pay Service.

11. All promotions from the rank of Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel to that of Brigade Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel, from the rank of Brigade Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel to that of Surgeon-Colonel, and from the rank of Surgeon-Colonel to that of Surgeon-Major-General, are given by selection for ability and merit.

Royal War-
rant, Art. 8.
Royal War-
rant, Art. 9.

12. On appointment as Honorary Physician or Honorary Surgeon to Her Majesty an Officer below the rank of Surgeon-Colonel shall be promoted to that rank.

13. For distinguished service in the field an Officer of the Indian Medical Service may be promoted from any rank to that next above it, remaining supernumerary in the higher rank until he would have been promoted in ordinary course.

TENURE OF OFFICE IN ADMINISTRATIVE GRADES.

Despatches to the Government of India, Nos. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, para. 31, and 150, dated 15th June, 1876. Despatch to the Government of India, No. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, para. 29.

14. The tenure of office of Surgeon-Major-Generals and Surgeon-Colonels is limited to five years.

15. Surgeon-Colonels if not disqualified by age, are eligible either for employment for a second tour of duty in the same grade, or for employment in the higher grade of Surgeon-Major-General by promotion thereto.

16. Absence on leave in excess of six months on medical certificate, or of four months on private affairs, involves forfeiture of appointment.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES.

India Army CIRC., 6th Feb. and 20th May, 1885.

17. The following are the rates of "Indian pay and allowances" which will be drawn by Officers hereafter appointed to the Indian Medical Service :—

Rank. Years' Service. Indian Pay and Allowances per mensem.
R. A. P.
When not holding an appointment carrying higher pay.
Brigade Surgeon Lieut.-Cols. and Surgeon-Lieut.-Cols. 25 and over 888 12 0
" 20 852 3 7
Surgeon-Majors 15 677 6 11
" 12 640 14 6
A.
When not receiving any Staff Allowance.
R. A. P.
Surgeon-Captains 10 451 14 5
" 6 433 10 2
" 5 335 12 2
Surgeon-Captains and Surgeon-Lieut. under 5 317 8 0
B.
When receiving Staff Allowance in addition. (See para. 18, and note on next p.)
R. A. P.
Surgeon-Captains 10 410 9 5
" 6 392 5 2
" 5 304 14 2
Surgeon-Captains and Surgeon-Lieut. under 5 256 10 0

18. On first appointment they will only come into receipt of "Indian pay and allowances" (Column A) from the date of their arrival in India.

19. Officers holding the principal administrative appointments and substantive military charges of the Indian Medical Service receive at present the following consolidated salaries:—

Rs. per Mensem.
Surgeon-Maj.-Gen., Bengal 2,700
" Madras 2,500
" Bombay 2,500
Surgeon-Col. From 2,250
to 1,800
Brigade Surgeon-Lieut.-Col. or
Surgeon-Lieut.-Col. in sub-
stantive charge* of a Native
regiment		 ... 1,000 with Rs. 90, horse allowance in Cavalry regiments.
Surgeon-Major in substantive
charge of ditto		 ... 800 with Rs. 90 ditto
Surgeon-Captain above 5 years'
full pay service in substan-
tive charge of a Native regi-
ment		 ... 600 with Rs. 60 ditto
Surgeon-Captain under 5 years'
ditto or Surgeon-Lieut. in
substantive charge of a Na-
tive regiment		 ... 450 with Rs. 60 ditto

20. The salaries of other substantive medical appointments in the Civil and Military Departments are consolidated, and vary from Rs. 1,800 to Rs. 400 per mensem.

21. Qualified officers of the Medical Service are also eligible for appointments in the Assay Department. The salaries of these appointments are from Rs. 600 to Rs. 2,250 per mensem.

22. Medical Officers are required to perform two years' regimental duty in India before they can be considered eligible for civil employment.

23. No Medical Officer, however employed, can receive any staff allowance in addition to the pay laid down in para. 16 unless he has passed the examination in Hindustani known as the "Lower Standard." The passing of this examination does not of itself bring any increase of pay to an Officer, unless appointed to a substantive or officiating charge; but failure to pass disqualifies an Officer, even when holding such substantive or officiating charge, from receiving any portion of the staff allowances of the appointment.

24. Surgeon Major-Generals and Surgeon-Colonels on vacating office at the expiration of the five years' tour of duty, will be permitted to draw in India respectively an unemployed salary of Rs. 1,200 per mensem in the former, and Rs. 900 in the latter case, for a period of six months from the date of their vacating office, after which they will be placed while unemployed on the following scale of pay per diem:—

Surgeon Major-General. Surgeon-Colonel.
£ s. d. £ s. d.
After 30 years service on full pay 2 5 0 1 14 0
" 25 " " " 2 5 0 1 10 0
" 20 " " or
on promotion should this period of service
not be completed		 2 0 0 1 8 0

LEAVE RULES.

25. †Officers of the Indian Medical Service, below the rank of Surgeon-Colonel may be granted:—

(1.) Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.

(2.) Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—

After arrival in India, on first appointment £200 a year.
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension... 250 "
Ditto, ditto, 10th ditto 300 "
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto 400 "
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto 450 "
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto 500 "

(3.) Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay and half the Staff salary of appointment.

26. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

27. An officer, unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence will, unless he is under para. 25 specially granted an extension of leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may require. An officer will also be liable to be placed on half pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India.

28. Leave may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief, or of the Local Governments in India in the case of officers not under the direct orders of a Commander-in-Chief.

29. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, will be required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

30. Officers of the Administrative Grades may be granted one period of leave not exceeding eight months during their tenure of appointment.

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 265, of 26th Sept., 1878.

Despatches to Govt. of India, No. 340, of 7th Nov., 1864, paras. 28 & 30, & No. 167, of 23rd Aug. 1866, para. 12. Order of Govt. of India (Home Dept.) No. 150, dated 15th March, 1880.

Despatch to Govt. of India, (Financial), No. 29, 27th Jan., 1876.

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 235, 16th Nov., 1866, para. 7.

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 340, dated 7th Nov., 1864, para. 33.

Despatch to India, No. 9, dated 15th April, 1886.

* See note on next page.

† Officers in Civil Employment, while they continue in such employment, are not affected by these rules.

HONOURS AND REWARDS.

31. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are eligible for the military distinction of the Order of the Bath, and for good service pensions.

32. Six of the most meritorious Officers are named Honorary Physicians, and six are named Honorary Surgeons, to Her Majesty.

RETIRING PENSIONS AND HALF-PAY.

33. Officers of the Indian Medical Service will be allowed to retire on the following scale of pension, on completion of the required periods of service:—

After 30 years' service for pension £
" 25 " " " " " " " " " " " 700
" 20 " " " " " " " " " " " 500
" 17 " " " " " " " " " " " 365
" 17 " " " " " " " " " " " 292

34. Time of service for pension will reckon from date of first commission, and will include all leave taken under the rules quoted in paras. 24 to 29.

35. A Surgeon-Major-General, after five years' active employment in India in that grade, will be entitled to retire upon a pension of £350 per annum, in addition to that which he may be entitled under the above scale.

36. A Surgeon-Colonel will, after five years' active employment in India in that appointment be entitled to retire upon a pension of £250 per annum, in addition to the pension to which he may be entitled under the above scale.

37. In each of the above cases stated in paras. 34 and 35, six months' absence on medical certificate will be allowed to count towards actual service in those grades. (See para. 29).

38. A Surgeon-Major-General or Surgeon-Colonel who has completed his term of service and has reverted to British pay, may reside in Europe, at the same time qualifying for higher pension.

39. With a view to maintain the efficiency of the service, all Medical Officers of the rank of Brigade Surgeon Lieut.-Col., Surgeon-Lieut.-Col. and Surgeon-Major shall be placed on the Retired List when they shall have attained the age of fifty-five years, and all Surgeons-General and Deputy Surgeons-General when they shall have attained the age of sixty years. In any special case, where it would appear to be for the good of the service that the Officer should continue in employment, he may be so continued, subject in each case to the sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

40. Medical Officers not entitled to pension, when placed on temporary or permanent half-pay under para. 26, will be granted the British rate of half-pay of the corresponding military rank as under:—

Rank. Rates of Half-pay.
Per Diem. Per Annum.
s. d. £ s. d.
Brigade Surgeon Lieut.-Col. ... .. 11 0 200 15 0
Surgeon-Lieut.-Col. ... .. 9 6 173 7 6
Surgeon-Major ... .. 7 0 127 15 0
Surgeon-Captain ... .. 3 0 54 15 0
Surgeon-Lieutenant ... ..

Officers cannot retire in India on half-pay (No. 54, 28th February 1865).

41. The period passed on half-pay will in no case be reckoned as service in calculating claims for promotion or to pension on retirement.

42. Officers hereafter admitted to the Indian Medical Service will, after retirement on pension, be liable to recall to military duty in case of any great emergency arising, up to limits of age laid down by the Government of India.

WOUND PENSIONS.

43. Medical Officers are entitled to the same allowances on account of wounds and injuries received in action as are granted to combatant Officers of Her Majesty's Indian Military Forces holding the corresponding military rank.

Note referred to on previous page: Substantive and Officiating Charges:—

Medical charges are of two kinds—substantive and officiating. The first is held by an Officer as his fixed and permanent appointment. For this he is entitled to the monthly consolidated pay laid down in the above scale (para. 18), subject to the condition specified in para. 22.

An officiating charge is held by an Officer who is acting temporarily in an appointment during the absence of the Officer whose substantive charge it is. The Officer thus officiating receives not the consolidated pay of the appointment laid down in the scale (para. 18), but the Indian pay and allowances (para. 16) of his own grade, plus half the staff allowance of the appointment in which he is officiating (plus half the staff pay of his own appointment, if he have one).

An Officer's staff allowance is the difference between his "Indian pay and allowances" (para. 16) and "consolidated pay" (para. 18) as given in the scales. The staff pay of an appointment is the difference between the Indian pay and allowances and the consolidated pay of the permanent incumbent. It has been ruled, however, that the extra allowance for an officiating charge shall never be less than Rs 100 per mensem.

Example 1. A Medical Officer of less than 5 years' service officiates for one in charge of a regiment of the same period of service. He receives Rs. 286. 10 + Rs. 100 = Rs. 386. 10.

Example 2. A Medical Officer of less than 5 years' service, officiating for a Surgeon-Captain of 6 years' service in charge of a regiment, receives Rs. 286. 10 + \frac{Rs. 600 - Rs. 392. 5. 2}{2} = Rs. 390 7. 5.

Example 3. A Surgeon-Captain of 5 years' service holding permanent medical charge of a regiment, and officiating for a Surgeon-Major absent from his regiment, receives Rs. 304. 14. 2. + \frac{800 - 677. 6. 11}{2} + \frac{600 - 304. 14. 2}{2} = Rs. 513. 11. 7.

Statutes of the Order of the Bath, and Despatch to the Government of India, No. 24, of 25th Jan., 1866.
Royal Warrant 7th Dec., 1891 Art. 11.
Despatch to Government of India, No. 112, dated 14th April, 1881.
Despatch to Government of India, No. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, paras. 36 to 38
Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 166, dated 24th June, 1869, para. 17.
Royal Warrant, 7th Dec., 1891 Art. 10.
Royal Warrant, dated 16th Nov. 1880, Art. 11
Despatch to Government of India, No. 112, dated 14th April, 1881.

Royal Warrant, 30th April, 1887, Art. 2.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

44. The claims to pension of widows and families of Medical Officers shall be treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pensions to the widows and families of British Officers as may be in force at the time being. (The Royal Warrant of 15th Nov., 1887, is the one now in force.)

45. The widows and families of Medical Officers are also entitled to pensions from the Indian Military Service Family Pension Fund, to which all Surgeons must, as a condition of their appointment, subscribe from the date of their arrival in India.

Despatch to the Government of India, No. 182, of the 30th June, 1866.

Family Pension Regulations.

INDIAN NURSING SERVICE.

CONDITIONS OF APPOINTMENT, &c.

1. The Nursing Establishment is to consist of two grades, viz.:-

  • (i) Lady Superintendents.
  • (ii) Nursing Sisters.

NOTE.—For convenience sake, the term “lady nurse” is used in the following paragraphs, where it is intended to include both the above grades.

2. Nursing Sisters must be, at the time of appointment, over 25 and under 35 years of age and in sound bodily health. They must have had at least three years' preliminary training and service combined, in a hospital in which adult male patients receive medical and surgical treatment, and in which a staff of Nursing Sisters is maintained.

3. The duration of a term of service, for both grades of lady nurses, shall be five years, capable of renewal for a further five years at the option of the Government, with the consent of the lady nurse.

4. The engagement may, however, be terminated at any time on six months' notice being given, either on the part of the Government or of the lady nurse (but see para. 13).

RATES OF PAY.

(In addition to free quarters, fuel, light, and punkah-pullers.)

5. For a Lady Superintendent ... ... .. ... ... ... ... ... Rs.
For a Nursing Sister ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... 300 per mensem.
commencing from the date of embarkation for India. 175 ,,

A Senior Nursing Sister, acting as Superintendent at a station where no Lady Superintendent is located, will receive an additional allowance of Rs. 25 per mensem.

FAMILY REMITTANCES.
